channel.title: Standor Blog
channel.description: Engineering insights and forensic techniques from the Standor team.

item.title: Entropy-Based Anomaly Detection: Finding Hidden C2 Traffic
item.description: Shannon entropy is one of the most powerful — and underused — signals in network forensics. A single byte-frequency distribution can tell you whether a payload is encrypted, compressed, or random with high confidence, no signature required.
item.author: team@standor.io (Standor Team)

item.title: TCP Stream Reconstruction: From Raw PCAP to Application Context
item.description: Reassembling a TCP session from raw packet captures is deceptively complex. Retransmissions, out-of-order delivery, RST storms, and selective acknowledgement all conspire to make naive concatenation wrong. Here is how Standor handles it.
item.author: team@standor.io (Standor Team)

item.title: CRDTs for Collaborative Forensics: Real-Time Annotation Without Conflicts
item.description: When two analysts annotate the same packet simultaneously, what happens? Without conflict resolution, one annotation silently overwrites the other. Standor uses Conflict-free Replicated Data Types to make concurrent edits safe by construction.
item.author: team@standor.io (Standor Team)

item.title: TLS Fingerprinting Without Decryption: JA3, JA4, and Beyond
item.description: You do not need to break encryption to fingerprint a TLS client. The ClientHello handshake message contains enough information to uniquely identify most TLS stacks — and by extension, the malware or software generating the traffic.
item.author: team@standor.io (Standor Team)
